title: Stress relaxation in fiber networks via force-dependent stochastic severing
authors: Prathamesh Kulkarni, Anatoly B. Kolomeisky, Fred C. MacKintosh
abstract: Fiber networks contribute to the mechanical stability of various biological systems, from cells to tissues. Such systems have been modeled by networks of springs or fibers that exhibit rigidity transitions as a function of either connectivity or applied strain. For a fiber network under constant applied strain, severing can reduce the connectivity and destabilize an initially rigid structure. Here, we investigate stress relaxation in spring and fiber networks in the presence of stochastic, force-dependent severing. A computational model to predict stress relaxation with mechanochemical feedback of stress on severing is developed. We also examine the effects of severing on the network topology and onset of rigidity transition. Using 2D triangular lattice-based computer simulations, we explore different limits of the feedback and demonstrate the shift in the onset of rigidity depending on the limit. The limit of tension-suppressed severing delays stress relaxation and shifts the transition into the bending-dominated regime to lower-than-expected connectivity. In contrast, tension-enhanced severing accelerates relaxation and shifts the transition to higher-than-expected connectivity. It is also found that the magnitude of this shift depends on the applied shear strain and the strength of the feedback. Our theoretical approach clarifies some microscopic aspects of these phenomena. Understanding the impact of such feedback mechanisms can provide valuable insights into designing systems by tuning the feedback to the desired response.
summary: This paper models stress relaxation in biological fiber networks subjected to random fiber severing whose rate depends on the local tension. By simulating different feedback mechanisms, the authors show that tuning this mechanochemical feedback can significantly alter the network's mechanical stability, shifting the critical point where the structure loses its rigidity. This provides insight into how biological systems might actively regulate their mechanical properties.
main_problem: The study investigates how stress relaxation occurs in fiber networks when the severing of fibers is stochastically dependent on the local mechanical stress.
main_result: The mechanochemical feedback mechanism significantly shifts the critical connectivity required for the network to maintain rigidity, delaying or accelerating the loss of mechanical integrity depending on whether severing is tension-suppressed or enhanced.
method: The authors developed and employed a computational model using 2D triangular lattice simulations and an event-driven simulation approach (Gillespie algorithm) to track stress and connectivity changes over time.
model_or_system: The system models fiber networks (like those in biological tissues) as spring networks subjected to applied shear strain. The model incorporates both stretching and bending energies, and the severing rate is governed by a feedback parameter ($c$) related to local tension.
key_observables: Stress relaxation ($\sigma(t)$), network connectivity ($Z(t)$), and the onset of the mechanical rigidity transition.
important_parameters: Applied shear strain ($\gamma$), network connectivity ($z$), and the feedback parameter ($c$) which defines tension-suppressed ($c<0$) or tension-enhanced ($c>0$) severing.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes fast fragmentation and relaxation timescales compared to the severing timescale, and the model simplifies by ignoring polymerization/depolymerization reactions.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the mechanical phase transition ($\gamma$ vs. $z$) and stress vs. time curves for different feedback limits, showing the shift in the critical transition point.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the physical model, develops the computational framework incorporating force-dependent severing, analyzes the stress relaxation dynamics under different feedback regimes, and concludes by discussing the implications for designing mechanically robust systems.
why_interesting: While focused on classical mechanics, the concept of non-equilibrium phase transitions, critical points, and the influence of local feedback mechanisms on macroscopic stability echoes principles found in open quantum systems and non-equilibrium statistical mechanics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: The first panel of the schematic demonstrates severing via cofilin in an actin filament. Severing occurs when cofilin binds to an actin filament, resulting in fila- ments with free barbed (plus) and pointed (minus) ends. These filaments can undergo rapid polymerization (at the barbed end) and depolymerization (at the pointed end) in the presence of ATP/ADP, depending on local actin monomer concentration. The second panel shows a sev- ering event in the context of an actin network. Fragmen- tation results in dangling ends and nonaffine structural rearrangement due to relaxation.

FIG. 2: (a) Diagram showing the mechanical phase tran- sition in fiber networks. Networks with central force in- teractions undergo a mechanical phase transition con- trolled by the applied shear strain γ and average connec- tivity z. In the absence of bending interactions, networks with a given connectivity z remain floppy when held un- der low applied shear strains and are rigid when held un- der high values of applied shear strains. With increasing applied strain, these networks exhibit a transition from a floppy to a rigid phase, and the transition, being critical in nature, can be depicted using the zc = zc(γ) curve in the figure. The mechanical transition is observed in the presence...

FIG. 3: (a) Stress relaxation σ and Average connectivity z as a function of time t for varying values of the ten- sion feedback parameter (positive, negative, and zero). (b) Variation of Stress σ and ratio of Bending energy HBend to Total energy HTotal with Connectivity z for the networks. The networks have a dimensionless bending rigidity of ˜κ = 10−5 and are held under an applied shear strain γ = 0.2. From Fig. 3a, it can be observed that connectivity z decreases with time due to the occurrence of severing events. For better physical understanding, stress σ can be analyzed with connectivity z, as shown in Fig. 3b, for the same networks. Connectivity z is plot- ted on the horizontal axis...

FIG. 4: Absence of feedback (c = 0): Stress σ and ra- tio of Bending energy HBend to Total energy HTotal with Connectivity z for the networks as a function of con- nectivity z for varying applied strain γ. Fig 4a and 4b correspond to behavior of networks with dimensionless bending rigidity of ˜κ = 10−4 and 10−5 respectively with no feedback present. Bending rigidity clearly affects the behavior near the phase transition, with higher bending rigidity making it more gradual. This is in agreement with fiber network behavior.

FIG. 5: Presence of Feedback: Stress σ and Nonaffinity Γ with connectivity z for the networks for varying applied strain γ. The top and bottom panels correspond to the behavior of networks with tension feedback parameter values of c = −50 and c = 50, respectively. For smaller strains, the behavior is similar to when the feedback is ab- sent. In the presence of moderately high strains, a clear shift in the transition can be observed in both tension- suppressed (c = −50) and tension-enhanced (c = 50) severing cases. The “shift” in the transition is depen- dent on the applied strain. The dramatic stress-drop aligns with a peak in Nonaffinity indicating the transi- tion into bending-dominated...
